# Research: management-hub-vs-walkable-world-patterns

*Auto-generated by AIDLC planning session*

**Question:** What are proven UX patterns in tycoon/management sims (Game Dev Tycoon, Two Point Hospital, Project Highrise, Mall Tycoon) for presenting a multi-location hub — clickable map vs walkable world — and what criteria determine when each works best for a small-team indie sim?

---

# Management Hub UX Patterns: Clickable Map vs Walkable World

## Executive Summary

Tycoon and management sims resolve the multi-location problem along a spectrum with three dominant archetypes: **static clickable hub** (Game Dev Tycoon, Software Inc. early game), **zoomable walkable world with camera authority** (Two Point Hospital, Project Highrise, RollerCoaster Tycoon), and **hybrid overview-plus-detail** (Theme Hospital's map screen + campus view, Rimworld's world map + colony). For a small-team indie mall sim with five distinct stores, the decision hinges on whether each store's *signature mechanic* demands spatial/diegetic interaction (walkable) or abstracted menu interaction (hub). Mallcore Sim's mechanics—refurbishment quality, pack opening, authentication, warranty upsell, late-fee cycles—are **menu-native**, which strongly favors a clickable hub with optional ambient walkable flavor.

---

## 1. The Pattern Spectrum

### 1.1 Pure Clickable Hub
**Exemplars:** Game Dev Tycoon, Kairosoft library (Game Dev Story, Mega Mall Story), Software Inc. (early phases), Gnomoria menus, Cultist Simulator.

**Defining traits:**
- Single screen or small set of screens; no avatar; no camera traversal.
- Locations are tiles, buttons, or cards arranged on a static backdrop.
- All interaction is pointer-driven; time advances via explicit tick control.
- Visual fidelity is low-to-medium; information density is high.

**Why it works:** The player's cognitive load is on *decisions*, not *wayfinding*. Clicking a store opens a modal or drawer; closing it returns instantly. No camera state to restore, no lost context.

**Cost of entry:** Low. One artist can ship a hub in weeks. No pathfinding, no agent AI, no collision.

### 1.2 Walkable / Zoomable World
**Exemplars:** Two Point Hospital, Project Highrise, Mall Tycoon (2002), Parkitect, Planet Coaster, Prison Architect.

**Defining traits:**
- Orthographic or isometric camera with pan, zoom, and sometimes rotation.
- Customers, staff, and objects are simulated agents visible on screen.
- Interaction happens in-world (click a counter, drag a wall) and in overlay panels.
- The world itself is the information display—queues visible as lines of sprites, sales visible as particle pops.

**Why it works:** Emergent visibility. Problems reveal themselves ("why is that queue so long?"). The sim becomes legible through watching, not reading. Screenshots sell the game.

**Cost of entry:** High. Requires tilemap/grid system, pathfinding, agent animation, a camera controller, LOD for zoom levels, and art assets at all those zoom levels. A small team can spend 6–12 months just on the walkable foundation before mechanics land.

### 1.3 Hybrid Overview + Detail
**Exemplars:** Theme Hospital (campus list → hospital view), Two Point Hospital (world map → single hospital), Rimworld (planet map → colony), Football Manager (league table → match engine), Fallout Shelter (vault overview → room detail).

**Defining traits:**
- Two distinct camera scales with an explicit transition (zoom-out or scene-swap).
- Overview is hub-like: status dots, alert badges, at-a-glance KPIs across all locations.
- Detail is walkable or semi-walkable: one location at full fidelity.
- Player can "live" in either view; alerts on the overview pull them down; "done here" returns them up.

**Why it works:** Separates strategic decisions (which store needs me?) from tactical decisions (what do I do there?). Prevents the walkable view from becoming an attention hog for stores that don't need attention.

**Cost of entry:** Medium-high. You pay for the walkable view *and* the hub, but the hub doubles as the main menu and pause context, so the marginal cost is smaller than it looks.

---

## 2. Case Studies

### 2.1 Game Dev Tycoon (Greenheart Games, 2012)
Two developers. Single office view, top-down, non-walkable; employees are stationary sprites at desks. All gameplay happens through menus launched from the office. The "office" is essentially a themed hub background. Shipped in ~9 months.

**Lesson:** When the sim's depth is in *decisions per tick* rather than *agents per screen*, a static hub carries the entire game. Players did not miss walkability; they praised pacing.

### 2.2 Two Point Hospital (Two Point Studios, 2018)
Studio of ~30 at launch. Fully walkable isometric hospital with simulated patients, staff, and illnesses. Also has a world-map overview of unlocked hospitals, each with star ratings and challenge state.

**Lesson:** Walkability is load-bearing because diseases are *visual gags* and queues are *spatial failures*. Remove the walkable layer and you remove 80% of the humor and half the feedback loop. But the world-map hub is what lets players *manage multiple hospitals without drowning*—each hospital is a separate scene, not one giant map.

### 2.3 Project Highrise (SomaSim, 2016)
Small team. Side-view skyscraper. Each floor is walkable; elevators and utility routing are spatial puzzles. No hub above the building—the building *is* the hub because it's vertically stacked and visible at a glance.

**Lesson:** When locations can be arranged on one continuous surface that fits a single camera, you don't need a separate hub. Verticality (or a long horizontal corridor) replaces the overview screen.

### 2.4 Mall Tycoon (Take-Two, 2002)
Top-down 3D walkable mall with stores as footprints. Customers pathfind between stores. Store interiors are abstracted—you don't walk *inside* a store; you click it to get a panel. Each store is a black box with a facade, inventory menu, and staff list.

**Lesson (most relevant to Mallcore):** Mall Tycoon already solved this exact problem in 2002 by making the mall walkable *between* stores and menu-based *within* stores. The store interior is a lie; only its exterior and customer flow are simulated. This hybrid—walkable mall corridor, menu-driven store interiors—is the proven template for "five stores in one mall."

### 2.5 Kairosoft's Mega Mall Story (2011)
Top-down grid where each store is a placed tile. The mall is technically walkable (customers move), but the camera is small, zoom is limited, and player interaction is almost entirely menu-driven. Stores are upgraded via panels, not by building interiors.

**Lesson:** You can get 90% of the "living mall" feeling from animated customer dots on a small map, without building true walkable interiors. The walkable layer is *ambient*, not interactive.

### 2.6 Theme Hospital / Two Point Hospital world map
The world map is a clickable hub where each hospital is a button with star count, cash, and reputation. The player picks one, loads into it, plays, returns. This is the closest analogue to a mall with five distinct stores *if each store were its own scene*.

---

## 3. Decision Criteria

Use these to decide where Mallcore Sim lands on the spectrum:

### 3.1 Criterion A — Where is the Mechanic Located?
- **In-menu mechanics** (authentication panel, pack-open animation, warranty-offer dialog, refurbishment grading UI): favor **hub**. A walkable world adds travel time between the same menu appearing in five places.
- **Spatial mechanics** (queue placement, shelf layout, sightlines, foot-traffic routing): favor **walkable**. These mechanics *require* a visible floor plan.

Mallcore's listed signature mechanics are all in-menu. Verdict: **hub-leaning**.

### 3.2 Criterion B — How Often Does the Player Switch Locations?
- **Many switches per minute** (each store demands attention every few ticks): hub minimizes travel cost.
- **Long dwell times per location** (spend 10 minutes optimizing one store, then move on): walkable is fine; the camera move is rare.

A five-store mall with simultaneous day-cycle pressure implies frequent switching. Verdict: **hub-leaning**.

### 3.3 Criterion C — How Much Does the Sim Sell Itself Visually?
- **Marketing depends on screenshots/GIFs of activity** (Planet Coaster, Two Point Hospital): walkable is marketing.
- **Marketing depends on aesthetic, narrative, or systems depth** (Cultist Simulator, Game Dev Tycoon): hub is enough if the art direction is strong.

A 2000s mall has *enormous* visual identity potential (neon, food court tile, fluorescent lighting, CRT window displays). An ambient walkable layer is marketing gold even if it's non-interactive. Verdict: **hybrid-leaning**.

### 3.4 Criterion D — Team Size and Scope Risk
- **1–3 devs, <18 months:** hub or hybrid with *non-interactive* walkable ambience.
- **4–8 devs, 2+ years:** hybrid with interactive walkable layer is feasible.
- **Full studio:** any pattern works.

Verdict: **hub or ambient-walkable hybrid**.

### 3.5 Criterion E — Save/Load and Scene Boundaries
- **One scene for the whole game:** walkable world (everything persists in one tree).
- **Per-location scenes:** hub is natural (each location is a scene, hub is a menu).

Mallcore has a 5-tier autoload init and signal-contract architecture—systems are global, scenes are views. Either pattern fits, but **per-store scenes with a hub scene** maps cleanly onto the existing separation.

### 3.6 Criterion F — Time Control Semantics
- **Global clock, all locations tick together** (mall day cycle): hub with "open day" button works; walkable also works.
- **Per-location time that can be paused independently**: hub is mandatory.

Mallcore has a shared day cycle. Verdict: neutral.

---

## 4. Anti-Patterns Observed

1. **The Empty Corridor.** A walkable hub with nothing to do between stores. Players resent the travel tax. *(Early-access builds of several unnamed mall sims.)* Fix: make the corridor itself carry mechanics (foot-traffic heatmap, kiosk placement, mall-wide events) or shrink the corridor to near-zero travel time.

2. **Modal Trap.** A walkable world that funnels every click into a full-screen modal. The walkable layer becomes decorative. Players ask "why isn't this a menu?" Fix: either commit to in-world interaction (click the counter, drag the shelf) or drop the walkable pretense.

3. **Two Cameras, One Mind.** A hybrid where the overview and detail show the *same data* at different zoom levels, so neither view is optimized. Fix: the overview must show *different* information (cross-store KPIs, alerts, portfolio health) from the detail view (inventory, staff, pricing).

4. **Hub Without Identity.** A clickable hub where the five stores look like five identical buttons. Wastes the setting. Fix: each store's hub card is a diorama, animates on hover, shows live activity (a customer walking out, a cash-register blink).

5. **Walkable Avatar With No Purpose.** A player-controlled manager sprite that walks the mall but whose position doesn't affect anything. Flavor without function. Fix: either tie the avatar to a mechanic (presence boosts staff morale in nearby stores) or remove it.

---

## 5. Recommendation for Mallcore Sim

**Adopt a hybrid: a clickable management hub as the primary interface, with an ambient walkable mall as the backdrop.**

Concretely:

### 5.1 The Hub (primary)
A top-down or ¾-perspective render of the mall concourse. Each of the five stores is a **diorama card**—a framed view of the storefront at rest, with live activity (customer silhouettes entering, neon sign flicker, register ding particles). Hovering reveals a KPI strip (today's revenue, inventory low flags, staff morale, active event). Clicking opens the **store drawer**: a side or bottom panel with the store's signature mechanic UI (pack opening, refurbishment queue, authentication bench, warranty dialog, late-fee roster).

**Why this maps to your architecture:**
- Each store drawer is a scene, loaded on demand; systems stay global via autoloads.
- Signals (`store_opened`, `drawer_closed`, `sale_completed`) keep the hub and drawers decoupled.
- PriceResolver remains the single price authority regardless of which drawer is open.

### 5.2 The Ambient Walkable Layer (flavor)
Customer sprites pathfind across the concourse between entrances and storefronts. They are *visual only*—the simulation of sales happens in systems, not in the sprite movement. The sprites are fed from `customer_system.gd` state, not the other way around. Removing the sprite layer must not change any gameplay outcome. This is the marketing layer and the nostalgia payoff.

### 5.3 The Day Summary (separate scene)
Already implied by your systems. Keep it as a dedicated modal scene launched by `day_closed`. This is your "close day → read summary" loop and shouldn't share screen real estate with the hub.

### 5.4 What to Explicitly Not Build
- **No walkable store interiors.** Mall Tycoon's 2002 decision holds: storefronts are black boxes with menu interiors.
- **No player avatar.** Adds scope without a mechanic to justify it.
- **No free-camera walkable concourse.** The concourse camera is fixed or has constrained pan/zoom for showing the ambient layer; it is not a first-class navigation mode.
- **No separate "overview world map" above the mall.** The mall *is* the overview. Adding a second layer above it duplicates the hub's job.

---

## 6. Implementation Sketch (Godot 4.6 specifics)

- **Hub scene** (`res://game/scenes/hub/mall_hub.tscn`): root `Node2D` with a background sprite, a `CustomerAmbientLayer` node (pooled `Sprite2D` customers driven by `customer_system` signals), five `StorefrontCard` nodes, a top status bar, and a `DrawerHost` `CanvasLayer`.
- **Storefront card** (`storefront_card.tscn`): a `Control` with a `SubViewport` rendering the diorama, a KPI strip, and a button area. Emits `storefront_clicked(store_id)`.
- **Drawer host** listens for `storefront_clicked`, instantiates the appropriate store drawer scene (`retro_games_drawer.tscn`, `pocket_creatures_drawer.tscn`, etc.), and slides it in.
- **Camera:** `Camera2D` with fixed position; small parallax on the ambient layer for depth. No player-driven pan. Zoom is cosmetic (1.0x → 1.05x on drawer open to focus attention).
- **Day cycle:** `day_cycle_controller` ticks regardless of which drawer is open. Closing a drawer does not pause; the player manages time via the existing day controls.
- **Save boundaries:** hub has no persistent state of its own beyond "which drawer was open" (optional UX polish). All persistence stays in the existing systems.

---

## 7. Summary Table

| Pattern | Best when | Team cost | Fits Mallcore? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure clickable hub | Mechanics are menu-native, frequent location switching | Low | Yes — strong baseline |
| Walkable world | Mechanics are spatial; screenshots sell the game | High | Partial — only for concourse flavor |
| Hybrid (overview + detail) | Many locations, per-location depth, strategic/tactical split | Medium-high | Overkill — there's only one mall |
| **Hub + ambient walkable (recommended)** | Menu-native mechanics + strong setting identity | Medium | **Best fit** |

The recommendation preserves your architectural invariants (signals, PriceResolver authority, per-scene drawers), keeps scope within small-team reach, and extracts the nostalgia/marketing value of the 2000s mall setting without paying the walkable-sim tax.
